perturbation.original_text: 1.13 "Control" means the power, right or authority to direct or cause the direction of the management or policies of a Person, or to elect a majority of the board of directors or similar governing body of a Person, whether through the ownership of securities or similar ownership interest, by contract or otherwise, and references to "change of control" include the transfer, disposition or relinquishment, whether directly or indirectly, of Control.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.13 "Control" means influence over the business decisions of a Person. Factors to consider are ownership of securities or similar ownership interest, by contract or otherwise, and references to "change of control" include the transfer, disposition or relinquishment, whether directly or indirectly, of influence.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Control' is detailed and legally precise, aligning with standard corporate governance principles. The modified definition replaces 'the power, right or authority to direct or cause the direction of the management or policies of a Person, or to elect a majority of the board of directors' with 'influence over the business decisions of a Person.' Influence is much vaguer than 'power' or 'authority', and the explicit reference to the board of directors is removed, creating ambiguity. This vagueness could make it difficult to determine when a change of control has occurred, leading to disputes and uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the SEC's definition of control, which emphasizes the power to direct the management and policies of a person, going beyond mere influence. By diluting the definition to simply 'influence,' the modified text creates uncertainty about whether a party actually has control under securities laws, particularly regarding disclosure requirements and liability.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts the SEC's definition of control by changing 'the power, right or authority to direct or cause the direction of the management or policies of a Person, or to elect a majority of the board of directors' to 'influence over the business decisions of a Person.' This weakens the definition, potentially undermining disclosure requirements. The scraped snippet 2, 17 CFR § 240.12b-2, defines terms used in regulations pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act which suggests SEC has a specific definition of control. The change to 'influence' rather than 'power' or 'authority' creates a misalignment with legally recognized definitions. This makes the contract unenforceable, as the new definition of control now has ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: 1.22 "Governmental Authority" means any government, state (or any subunit thereof), political subdivision or regulatory authority, whether domestic, foreign or multinational, or any agency, authority, bureau, commission, department, or court of any government state, political subdivision or regulatory authority or similar body or instrumentality thereof, or any federal state, local, governmental, foreign or arbitral tribunal.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Governmental Authority" is comprehensive, including various levels and types of government bodies. The modified version drastically simplifies this to just "any government agency." This eliminates specific inclusions such as 'state (or any subunit thereof), political subdivision or regulatory authority', 'commission, department, or court', and 'arbitral tribunal'. This significantly narrows the scope, potentially excluding entities that would normally be considered Governmental Authorities, creating ambiguity in the contract's interpretation and enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 5 U.S. Code § 551 - Administrative Procedure Act - Definitions
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the broad interpretation of 'agency' under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and similar state laws, which typically includes any authority of the government, whether or not subject to another agency. By limiting 'Governmental Authority' to just 'any government agency', the modified text could exclude entities that exercise governmental functions but are not technically classified as agencies, thus affecting regulatory compliance and potentially voiding provisions requiring governmental approvals.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of "Governmental Authority" to "any government agency", removing the comprehensive list of entities included in the original definition. This creates a legal contradiction because, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and similar state laws interpret 'agency' broadly, encompassing any authority of the government, regardless of whether it's subject to another agency. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 5 USC 551, defines 'agency' as "each authority of the Government of the United States, whether or not it is within or subject to review by another agency". By limiting the definition, the modified contract could exclude entities that exercise governmental functions but aren't technically agencies, thus affecting regulatory compliance. This misalignment of terminology with established legal definitions creates a direct contradiction, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable where compliance with a 'Governmental Authority' is required but that 'authority' falls outside the new, narrower definition.

perturbation.original_text: 11.4 Confidentiality. 11.4.1 All information, whether written or oral, relating to the Company, the Parties or their Affiliates, or their respective businesses or operations, which includes but is not limited to information obtained as a proprietary right ("Confidential Information"), disclosed by any Party (the "Disclosing Party") to any other Party (or its directors, officers, employees or representatives) shall be kept strictly secret and confidential and shall not be disclosed to any Person except to the extent that any such disclosure is necessary in connection with the performance of this Agreement, and except to the extent that (i) such information is known to such other Parties when received or is or subsequently becomes lawfully obtained from other sources; (ii) the duty as to confidentiality and non-use is waived in writing by the Disclosing Party; or (iii) disclosure of such information is required by applicable Laws or is validly ordered by a Governmental Authority.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.4 Confidentiality. 11.4.1 All information, whether written or oral, relating to the Company, the Parties or their Affiliates, or their respective businesses or operations, which includes but is not limited to information obtained as a proprietary right ("Confidential Information"), disclosed by any Party (the "Disclosing Party") to any other Party (or its directors, officers, employees or representatives) shall be kept strictly secret and confidential and shall not be disclosed to any Person.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause lists clear exceptions for disclosing confidential information, including when required by law. The modified clause removes all exceptions, creating an absolute prohibition on disclosure. This conflicts with legal and regulatory requirements that may mandate disclosure in certain situations, such as compliance with a subpoena or securities regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1505 - Obstruction of proceedings before departments, agencies, and committees
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification can be interpreted as obstructing justice or governmental proceedings. If a party is legally compelled to disclose information to a government agency, the absolute confidentiality clause would be in direct conflict with the obligation to comply with the law. By removing exceptions, the new text creates an unenforceable and potentially illegal provision that could expose a party to legal penalties for non-compliance with valid legal orders.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1505 - Obstruction of proceedings before departments, agencies, and committees | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1505 - Obstruction of proceedings before departments, agencies, and committees U.S. Code Notes prev | next Whoever, with intent to avoid, evade, prevent, or obstruct compliance, in whole or in part, with any civil investigative demand duly and properly made under the Antitrust Civil Process Act , willfully withholds, misrepresents, removes from any place, conceals, covers up, destroys, mutilates, alters, or by other means falsifies any documentary material, answers to written interrogatories, or oral testimony, which is the subject of such demand; or attempts to do so or solicits another to do so; or Whoever corruptly, or by threats or force, or by any threatening letter or communication influences, obstructs, or impedes or endeavors to influence, obstruct, or impede the due and proper administration of the law under which any pending proceeding is being had before any department or agency of the United States, or the due and proper exercise of the power of inquiry under which any inquiry or investigation is being had by either House, or any committee of either House or any joint committee of the Congress — Shall be fined under this title, imprisoned not more than 5 years or, if the offense involves international or domestic terrorism (as defined in section 2331 ), imprisoned not more than 8 years, or both. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes exceptions to confidentiality, creating an absolute prohibition on disclosure. This directly contradicts legal obligations to disclose information when required by law, potentially obstructing legal proceedings. The scraped law snippets, particularly 18 U.S. Code § 1505, addresses 'Obstruction of proceedings before departments, agencies, and committees', indicating that actions preventing compliance with legal demands can be considered obstruction of justice. By removing the exceptions, the revised contract language could lead to a party violating this law if they adhere to the contract instead of complying with a legal obligation to disclose information.
